A call to hear, from heaven’s well.  
“I am ready,” the voice does cry,  
“To hear the plan from on high.”  

Come, little ladies, step inside,  
Where shadows and secrets choose to hide.  
Beyond this door, truth waits to see,  
The plan your Father gave to me.  

Enter, dare you, into this night,  
Where the sacred whispers ignite.  
A world unveiled, dark and grand—  
Come, and know the Father’s hand.

Having gone on a Mormon mission myself as a sister missionary (I don't practice the religion anymore), I will admit there were times that I had fears something like this would happen because we were in the bible belt and in at least one town we were in all the churches in the area were actively against us, it was kinda scary, we were just 2 young girls far away from family and friends with basically no contact with the regular world, that in itself was already scary, add on legitimately hostile locals, ugh, no way, never again. So, even if you don't agree with them, those missionaries are literally children, youngest are 18, it's usually there first time away from home, you don't have to let them teach you anything, I would actually say don't let them teach you anything, it's all propaganda anyways, but be nice to them, give them water if they are outside in the heat, or a snack bar or something,
I don’t think it’s propaganda but I respect your view and I think it’s great that you’re still so respectful about it.

Trying to read what he's got on that notepad. What on earth is Monopoly meant to mean?

I saw the film at TIFF, it was one of my favorites that I saw. I thought it was great. I won't say too much so I don't give away any spoilers but 'Monopoly' on the notepad is in fact referring to the board game. It was a great scene. If you want more explanation I can give it but didn't want to just outright say it in case you didn't want to know. It kind of plays into the bigger theme and discussion of the movie. The notepad is his schedule of how he wants things to proceed with the 2 girls once they arrive.
@jasonstamp10 I'd outright like to know. Thank you kindly.

@gegeuncle5382 OK, spoilers ahead....














from the best I can remember (it's been almost a month now since I saw it). He lures the girls deeper into the house after they try escaping through the front door but can't, into a room with 2 doors (the doors you can see in the trailer where he writes Belief and Disbelief). There's an area beside the doors with bookshelves that have books and some other things. I can't quite remember now the order that it happened in but I think it's this first, he pulls out the Monopoly board game and puts it on a table. He then also pulls out a copy of the board game called I think The Landlord's Game which came out in 1904, 31 years before Monopoly. He goes into the history of the Landlord's Game and how Parker Brothers basically stole the idea and turned it into Monopoly which became way more successful, with the creator of the Landlords Game not getting any credit and people forgetting about it in favor of Monopoly. He goes on to explain how Monopoly is an evolutionary iteration of the Landlord Game, how some things spawn new versions of things that are a bit different but essentially the same. He then laid out 3 symbols of the Abrahamic religions. It was the cross, the Jewish star of David, and I think the Islamic star and crescent. He goes into the history of how religions spin off into different iterations of essentially the same thing. Many come from the same baseline and then factions spin off and create a different iteration of the religion that they believe is more "correct and true". Many people in these different religions all think they are believing in the "right" religion, the correct one and he's trying to make the 2 girls understand this when they speak about Mormonism being the correct religion he should convert to. He's trying to make them question their blind faith in something, it's a larger discussion and theme throughout the film. As he's explaining the history of the board game and of the different religious histories, he's very menacing and you can feel the screw tightening on the situation.
